ch1.title: Chapter 1: Welcome to English Home Language

blocks.text: English Home Language helps learners read, write, listen, speak, and think with confidence. In this book, you will explore stories, information texts, vocabulary, grammar, and writing activities.
Each chapter is designed to help you grow step by step. You will read carefully, notice language patterns, build your [[vocabulary]], and improve your [[comprehension]].

blocks.alt: Learners reading and writing in class
blocks.caption: English helps learners read, write, speak, and understand the world around them.

blocks.text: Good readers do more than say words correctly. They think about meaning, ask questions, and connect what they read to what they already know.

vocabulary.definition: The words a person knows and understands.

comprehension.definition: The ability to understand what you read or hear.

worksheet.title: Getting Started
worksheet.prompt:
1. What do you think you will learn in English Home Language?

2. Why is reading important?

3. Write one sentence about how you want to improve this year.

blocks.text: Stories help us imagine people, places, and events. When we read a story, we learn to follow [[characters]], understand the [[setting]], and notice the [[plot]].
A character is a person or animal in a story. The setting is where and when the story takes place. The plot is the series of events that happen in the story.

blocks.alt: Story elements chart showing characters, setting and plot
blocks.caption: Characters, setting, and plot are important parts of a story.

blocks.text: As you read, ask yourself: Who is in the story? Where does it happen? What problem do the characters face? How is the problem solved?

characters.definition: The people or animals in a story.

setting.definition: The place and time in which a story happens.

plot.definition: The events that happen in a story.

worksheet.title: Understanding Story Elements
worksheet.prompt:
1. What is a character?

2. What is a setting?

3. Explain what plot means in your own words.

blocks.text: Not all texts are stories. Some texts are written to inform. These are called [[information texts]]. They help readers learn facts about a topic.
Information texts often include headings, pictures, labels, captions, and key facts. These features help readers find and understand important ideas.

blocks.caption: Information texts often use headings, labels, and captions.

blocks.text: When reading an information text, look for the [[main idea]] and the details that support it.

information texts.definition: Texts written to give facts and explain ideas.

main idea.definition: The most important point in a paragraph or text.

worksheet.title: Facts and Features
worksheet.prompt:
1. What is an information text?

2. Name two features of an information text.

3. Why do headings and pictures help readers?

blocks.text: Vocabulary grows when learners read, listen, speak, and write often. The more words you know, the easier it becomes to understand texts and explain your ideas.
A [[synonym]] is a word with a similar meaning. An [[antonym]] is a word with an opposite meaning.
For example, happy and joyful are synonyms. Happy and sad are antonyms.

synonym.definition: A word with the same or nearly the same meaning as another word.

antonym.definition: A word with the opposite meaning of another word.

worksheet.title: Words and Meanings
worksheet.prompt:
1. What is a synonym?

2. What is an antonym?

3. Write one pair of synonyms and one pair of antonyms.

blocks.text: A [[noun]] names a person, place, animal, or thing. A [[pronoun]] takes the place of a noun so that we do not repeat the same word too many times.
For example: Naledi reads her book. She enjoys it. In this example, the word she is a pronoun because it replaces Naledi.

blocks.caption: Pronouns help replace nouns in sentences.

noun.definition: A word that names a person, place, animal, or thing.

pronoun.definition: A word that takes the place of a noun.

worksheet.title: Naming Words
worksheet.prompt:
1. What is a noun?

2. What is a pronoun?

3. Rewrite this sentence using a pronoun: Sipho is running because Sipho is late.

blocks.text: A [[verb]] shows action or state of being. An [[adjective]] describes a noun.
For example: The small dog runs quickly. In this sentence, small is an adjective and runs is a verb.
Adjectives make writing more interesting because they give more detail. Verbs show what is happening.

verb.definition: A word that shows action or state of being.

adjective.definition: A word that describes a noun.

worksheet.title: Actions and Descriptions
worksheet.prompt:
1. What does a verb do?

2. What does an adjective do?

3. Write one sentence that includes both a verb and an adjective.

blocks.text: A sentence is a group of words that makes complete sense. Every sentence begins with a capital letter and ends with [[punctuation]].
A full stop ends a statement. A question mark ends a question. An exclamation mark shows strong feeling.

blocks.caption: Punctuation helps readers understand sentences clearly.

punctuation.definition: Marks used in writing to show meaning and structure, such as full stops and question marks.

worksheet.title: Writing Correct Sentences
worksheet.prompt:
1. Why is punctuation important?

2. What mark ends a question?

3. Write one statement and one question.

blocks.text: A paragraph is a group of sentences about one main idea. Good paragraphs stay on topic and include supporting details.
When writing a paragraph, begin with a clear sentence about the topic. Then add details that explain or support the idea.
Finally, read your work again and check spelling, punctuation, and meaning.

paragraph.definition: A group of sentences that stay on one topic.

worksheet.title: Writing a Paragraph
worksheet.prompt:
1. What is a paragraph?

2. Why should all the sentences in a paragraph stay on one topic?

3. Write a short paragraph about your favourite place.

blocks.text: Listening and speaking are important language skills. Good listeners pay attention, think carefully, and respond respectfully.
Good speakers use clear words, an audible voice, and complete ideas. They also listen to others before replying.
When learners discuss a topic, ask questions, or present to the class, they are practising listening and speaking skills.

audible.definition: Loud enough to be heard clearly.

worksheet.title: Speaking with Confidence
worksheet.prompt:
1. What makes a good listener?

2. What makes a good speaker?

3. Why is respectful listening important in class?

ch10.title: Chapter 10: Becoming a Strong Language Learner

blocks.text: You have learned about stories, information texts, vocabulary, grammar, writing, listening, and speaking. All these skills work together.
Strong language learners read often, ask questions, practise writing, and keep improving. They understand that learning takes time.
The more you read and use language, the stronger and more confident you become.

language learner.definition: A person who is developing skill in reading, writing, speaking, and understanding language.

worksheet.title: Looking Back
worksheet.prompt:
1. Name two important things you learned from this book.

2. Which chapter helped you the most, and why?

3. How will you keep improving your English this year?
